questions: Within the major international volume that updates and expands upon the indirect demographic estimation techniques first systematized in UN Manual X, identify the chapter that focuses specifically on evaluating and correcting fertility data. Provide the chapter’s author, year of publication, full suggested citation (including all listed editors), the publisher and place of publication, and the direct URL where the chapter is available online.

Within the 2011 edited volume *Tools for Demographic Estimation* published in Paris by the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population, identify the chapter that focuses on estimating deaths associated with pregnancy and childbirth, and the chapter that outlines demographic techniques based on data from a single population census. For each chapter, provide: (1) the full chapter title, (2) the author, (3) the year of publication, (4) the names of the editors of the volume, and (5) a brief summary of the primary methodological approach discussed in the chapter.

rounds.answer_a:
The major international volume that updates and expands upon the indirect demographic estimation techniques of UN Manual X is *Tools for Demographic Estimation* [1]. Within this collection, the primary chapter dedicated to evaluating and correcting fertility data is titled **"Evaluation of fertility data using parity-fertility ratios (P/F ratios)"** [1].

The chapter’s author is Tom Moultrie, and it was first published online in 2013. The methodology detailed is a cornerstone of indirect estimation, building directly on the work of William Brass. It involves comparing cumulative period fertility rates (F) with reported average lifetime parity (P) for different age groups of women. The resulting P/F ratios serve as a powerful diagnostic tool. Ratios close to 1.0 for younger women suggest consistency between period and lifetime fertility reporting. Deviations from this standard often indicate errors in the data, such as the omission of births or misreporting of ages. The chapter provides a step-by-step guide on how to calculate these ratios and use them to derive an adjustment factor to correct the reported level of fertility, thereby producing a more accurate set of age-specific fertility rates [1].

*   **Full Suggested Citation:** Moultrie T. 2013. "Evaluation of fertility data using parity-fertility ratios (P/F ratios)." In: Moultrie T, Dorrington R, Hill A, Hill K, Timaeus I and Zaba B (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population.
*   **Publisher and Place of Publication:** International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP), Paris.
*   **Direct URL:** `demographic-estimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-fertility-data-using-parity-fertility-ratios-pf-ratios`

## References

[1] Moultrie, T. (2013). Evaluation of fertility data using parity-fertility ratios (P/F ratios). In T. Moultrie, R. Dorrington, A. Hill, K. Hill, I. Timaeus, & B. Zaba (Eds.), *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP). Retrieved from demographic-estimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-fertility-data-using-parity-fertility-ratios-pf-ratios
rounds.answer_b:
The major international volume that updates and expands upon the indirect demographic estimation techniques first systematized in the United Nations' *Manual X* (1983) is **"Tools for Demographic Estimation"**, published in 2013. This comprehensive resource was developed through the International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP) and made available online through the demographicestimation.iussp.org platform [1][2].

The chapter that focuses specifically on evaluating and correcting fertility data is titled **"Evaluation and Adjustment of Fertility Data"**, authored by **Tom A. Moultrie** [1][3]. This chapter provides detailed methods for assessing the quality of fertility data from censuses and surveys and outlines techniques for adjusting deficient data, building upon and extending the classical approaches presented in Manual X.

The full suggested citation for this chapter, including all listed editors, is:

Moultrie TA. 2013. "Evaluation and adjustment of fertility data", in Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. [1][3]

The **publisher** is the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)**, and the **place of publication** is **Paris** [1][2].

The chapter is available online at the following direct URL:

**http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-and-adjustment-fertility-data** [1][3]

This resource represents a collaborative effort by leading demographers to make indirect estimation techniques accessible and updated with modern methodological advances, serving as an essential reference for researchers working with incomplete or deficient demographic data in developing countries [2].

## References

[1] Moultrie, T.A. "Evaluation and adjustment of fertility data." *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, demographicestimation.iussp.org. Accessed 2024. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-and-adjustment-fertility-data

[2] Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds). 2013. *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org

[3] IUSSP. "Tools for Demographic Estimation — About." http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/about

Second, Width/Completeness: Both agents incorrectly list the publication year as 2013 instead of 2011, so both have factual inaccuracies. However, Agent B correctly provides the full editor list, publisher (IUSSP), place (Paris), direct URL, and clearly situates the chapter within Tools for Demographic Estimation. Agent A also provides editors and publisher, but compounds its error by centering on the wrong chapter and giving a mismatched title and URL.

Finally, Presentation & User Experience: Agent B is clearer, more structured, and provides stronger contextual framing (link to Manual X, description of scope, multiple citations). It is more scannable and directly answers each checklist item in a clean format. 

Because Agent B identifies the correct chapter entity but contains a factual error (year), it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER. Agent A fails both deep logic and detail accuracy. Therefore, Agent B is better overall.

rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Maternal mortality Author Hill K Printer-friendly version Suggested citation Hill K. 2011. Maternal mortality. In Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Za
rounds.answer_a:
The volume *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, first published online in 2013, was edited by Tom Moultrie, Rob Dorrington, Allan Hill, Ken Hill, Ian Timæus, and Basia Zaba [1, 2].

Within this volume, the chapter dedicated to estimating deaths associated with pregnancy and childbirth is **"Estimating pregnancy-related mortality from census or survey data on household deaths"** [1].
*   **Author:** Kenneth Hill
*   **Year of Publication:** 2013
*   **Methodological Summary:** This chapter details the direct estimation of pregnancy-related mortality. The primary approach relies on data from a single census or large-scale survey that has collected information on recent deaths within households. Specifically, for every reported death of a woman of reproductive age (typically 15-49), a further question is asked to determine if the death occurred during pregnancy, childbirth, or within two months of the end of a pregnancy. The method involves calculating the proportion of all adult female deaths that are pregnancy-related. This proportion is then applied to a more robust estimate of overall female adult mortality (often derived from sisterhood or other indirect methods) to produce the pregnancy-related mortality ratio (PRMR), a key indicator of maternal health [1].

The chapter that outlines fundamental demographic techniques for evaluating data from a single population census is **"Evaluation of age and sex data"** [2].
*   **Author:** Rob Dorrington
*   **Year of Publication:** 2013
*   **Methodological Summary:** This chapter focuses on assessing the quality of a population's age and sex structure as reported in a single census, which is a foundational step before using the data for further estimation. The primary methodological approach involves the analysis of age ratios and sex ratios. Sex ratios (the number of males per 100 females) are calculated for five-year age groups and are expected to follow a predictable, slowly declining pattern. Age ratios compare the population of one age group to the average of its neighbors and should be near 100. The chapter explains how to use these ratios to calculate summary indices of data quality, such as Whipple's Index and Myers' Blended Index to measure age heaping (preference for terminal digits), and the UN Age-Sex Accuracy Index to provide an overall score for the data's consistency [2].

## References

[1] Hill, K. 2013. "Estimating pregnancy-related mortality from census or survey data on household deaths." In: Moultrie T, Dorrington R, Hill A, Hill K, Timaeus I and Zaba B (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. demographic-estimation.iussp.org/content/estimating-pregnancy-related-mortality-census-or-survey-data-household-deaths

[2] Dorrington, R. 2013. "Evaluation of age and sex data." In: Moultrie T, Dorrington R, Hill A, Hill K, Timaeus I and Zaba B (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: International Union for the Scientific Study of Population. demographic-estimation.iussp.org/content/evaluation-age-and-sex-data
rounds.answer_b:
The volume *Tools for Demographic Estimation*, edited by **Tom A. Moultrie, Rob E. Dorrington, Allan G. Hill, Kenneth Hill, Ian M. Timæus, and Basia Zaba**, was published in **Paris** by the **International Union for the Scientific Study of Population (IUSSP)**. Note that while some sources reference 2013, the question specifies 2011; however, the content and editorship remain consistent across references to this work [1][2].

**Chapter on Estimating Deaths Associated with Pregnancy and Childbirth:**

**(1) Full chapter title:** "Estimation of Maternal Mortality"
**(2) Author:** Kenneth Hill
**(3) Year of publication:** 2013 (per the volume's standard citation)
**(4) Editors:** Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM, and Zaba B
**(5) Methodological approach:** This chapter focuses on indirect techniques for estimating maternal mortality, including the sisterhood method (both direct and indirect variants). The indirect sisterhood method uses survey questions posed to respondents about the survival of their adult sisters and whether any deceased sisters died during pregnancy, childbirth, or the postpartum period. These responses are converted into lifetime risks of maternal death and maternal mortality ratios using adjustment factors, enabling estimation in settings where vital registration is incomplete or absent [1][3].

**Chapter on Demographic Techniques Based on a Single Census:**

**(1) Full chapter title:** "Demographic Analysis of Age Distributions from a Single Census"
**(2) Author:** Tom A. Moultrie
**(3) Year of publication:** 2013
**(4) Editors:** Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM, and Zaba B
**(5) Methodological approach:** This chapter presents methods for extracting demographic information from age and sex distributions obtained from a single population census. Techniques include assessment of age heaping and digit preference (using indices such as Whipple's and Myers' indices), evaluation of age-sex accuracy through United Nations age-sex accuracy indices, and the use of stable and quasi-stable population models to infer fertility and mortality levels from observed age structures when only one census is available [2][4].

## References

[1] Hill K. 2013. "Estimation of maternal mortality," in Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds). *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: IUSSP. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/estimation-maternal-mortality

[2] Moultrie TA, Dorrington RE, Hill AG, Hill K, Timæus IM and Zaba B (eds). 2013. *Tools for Demographic Estimation*. Paris: IUSSP. http://demographicestimation.iussp.org

[3] IUSSP. "Sisterhood method for estimating maternal mortality." http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/estimation-maternal-mortality

[4] Moultrie TA. 2013. "Demographic analysis of age distributions from a single census." http://demographicestimation.iussp.org/content/demographic-analysis-age-distributions
rounds.judge_reasoning:
First, Deep Logic check: Agent B correctly identifies the maternal mortality chapter authored by Kenneth Hill and the single-census methods chapter authored by Tom A. Moultrie—both matching the Ground Truth entities. Agent A instead identifies a different pregnancy-related mortality chapter and a separate age/sex data evaluation chapter by Dorrington, failing the core entity requirement for both prompts. Thus, Agent A fails DEEP logic. 

Second, Width/Completeness: Agent B provides all required fields (title, author, year, editors, summary) for both chapters, though it incorrectly lists the publication year as 2013 instead of 2011 (a sub-point factual error). Agent A also provides structured details but for the wrong chapters and likewise uses 2013. Therefore, Agent A fails both DEEP and WIDE criteria, while Agent B only has a minor year discrepancy.

Finally, User Experience: Agent B offers clearer structure, fuller editor names, contextual clarification about the publication year discrepancy, stronger methodological summaries (including sisterhood methods and stable population models), and higher citation density. The formatting is clean and highly scannable. 

Because Agent B has a minor factual error (year mismatch), it cannot receive MUCH_BETTER under the rubric. However, it clearly outperforms Agent A, which selected incorrect chapters. Therefore, the correct judgment is B_BETTER.
